serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of muah chee is approximately 6 pieces (about 120 g), as sold by Sinpopo Coffee at SGD 6.00.

nutrient_source.title: Muah Chee (Glutinous Rice Cake with Peanut) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists muah chee with peanut coating. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo per-100g data for glutinous rice cake with peanut (muah chee). Where HPB Foodo does not report a micronutrient, the value is set to -1. Serving size of 120 g (6 pieces) is estimated from typical commercial portion; HPB Foodo reference portion is 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Rice-based Snack Products — analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for muah chee with peanut coating. Glutinous rice (sticky rice) has a published GI of approximately 86–98 in the University of Sydney GI Database. However, muah chee is a composite food: the glutinous rice dough is steamed and mixed with oil and sugar, and the peanut coating contributes fat and protein which attenuate glycemic response. An analogous estimate of GI 60 (medium) is applied, consistent with published GI values for glutinous rice-based snacks with fat/protein modifiers as documented in peer-reviewed literature (Björck et al., 1994, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition; Foster-Powell et al., 2002, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 57.0) / 100 = 34.2, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 47.5 × 1.2 = 57.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content at approximately 85 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Muah chee with peanut coating has a relatively low sodium content (~102 mg per 120 g serving), making it an acceptable occasional snack for individuals managing hypertension, provided overall daily sodium intake remains within the recommended 2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~60) and high carbohydrate content from glutinous rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Muah chee has a medium estimated GI of 60 and delivers approximately 57 g of carbohydrate per standard 6-piece serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and pair with a low-GI meal to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content with predominantly unsaturated fat from peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The peanut coating provides predominantly unsaturated fatty acids (mono- and polyunsaturated), and saturated fat is low at approximately 1.8 g per 100 g, making muah chee a relatively heart-friendly snack when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Muah chee (麻糍) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese glutinous rice snack made by steaming a dough of glutinous rice flour, water, and oil until it becomes soft, chewy, and translucent. The cooked dough is then cut or pulled into small bite-sized pieces and tossed generously in a mixture of finely ground roasted peanuts, sugar, and sometimes sesame seeds, so that each piece is fully coated. The result is a snack with a distinctively soft, sticky, and yielding texture contrasted by the crunchy, nutty, and slightly sweet peanut coating. Muah chee is a beloved hawker and coffeeshop snack in Singapore, commonly sold in portions of 6 pieces. Sinpopo Coffee, a heritage-inspired Singaporean café chain, serves muah chee as part of its traditional Singaporean dessert menu. Nutritionally, muah chee is a moderate-energy snack (~310 kcal per 100 g) with a significant carbohydrate load from the glutinous rice base and a meaningful contribution of unsaturated fat and protein from the peanut coating. The estimated glycemic index is medium (~60), moderated by the fat and protein in the peanut coating relative to plain glutinous rice. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is dairy-free. Individuals with peanut or sesame allergies must avoid this dish. For those managing Type 2 Diabetes, portion control is advised due to the high carbohydrate content per serving.

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo is an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes and lists muah chee with peanut coating. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data. GI is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for muah chee exists in the published literature. Serving size is estimated from commercial context (6 pieces at Sinpopo Coffee). Confidence is medium rather than high due to the GI estimation and serving-size estimation.

Minor micronutrients not reported by HPB Foodo are set to -1. 2. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 4. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 8. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. 10. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — muah chee is made from glutinous rice flour, oil, and peanuts with no hydrogenated fats; trans fat is confirmed negligible per HPB Foodo). 11. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based; no animal-derived cholesterol-containing ingredients are present). 12. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for muah chee exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. Serving size of 120 g (6 pieces) is estimated from the field survey listing description ('6 pieces for $6'). 14. Sinpopo Coffee - Funan is the sole restaurant source in the input record.
